%% the auto-generated tables in article/tables/ or the figures in article/figures/ (verify, don't
%% recall). Fences: enumerate RQ1 cells (never "significant across all datasets"); scope qbq vs
%% whole-exam = "not significant, not equivalence" (no TOST); RQ2 scoped to Qwen3.5; RQ6 anchor =
%% corroboration only, never winner; PT-CS hybrid rule per Method (full-N + V column in tables,
%% verified-only in figures and absolute levels).

\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}

All tables in this section are generated directly by the analysis code from the run stores. PT-CS
follows the reporting rule of Section~\ref{sec:datasets}: full data as primary for paired contrasts,
with a verified-stratum robustness column (marked V); verified only for absolute levels and transfer;
figures show the verified stratum.

\subsection{RQ1: Reasoning Trades Consistency for Agreement}
\label{sec:rq1}

\input{tables/tab_rq1_reasoning}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig1_rq1_twodim.pgf}
\caption{RQ1 in two dimensions: agreement change ($\Delta$QWK, off$\to$on) against consistency
change ($\Delta$ within-item SD) per dataset and model, on the paired clean items. Most points sit
in the upper half: reasoning costs consistency whether or not it buys agreement. Basis: PT-CS points
are the verified stratum (figures rule, Section~\ref{sec:datasets}).}
\label{fig:rq1}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig2_cost_vs_agreement.pgf}
\caption{Agreement gain against the reasoning token premium (log scale), per dataset and model.
The large gains concentrate at the expensive right end (Qwen); the cheap left end buys little.
Same basis as Fig.~\ref{fig:rq1}.}
\label{fig:cost}
\end{figure}

Reasoning is evaluated on the paired sampled items, restricted to the \emph{tractable} items: those
whose reasoning runs never overflowed the 32{,}768-token output ceiling, so a clean on-score exists.
The overflow exclusion removes 337 run rows (222 distinct items: 91 SemEval, 65 Mohler, 49 PT-CS, 17
RIAYN), all from Qwen with reasoning on; the extractable rate $\pi$ is 1.0 everywhere else. Because
the overflowing items are the ones provoking the longest reasoning, the excluded items are not
random, and every claim in this subsection holds for the tractable items only.

Agreement gains are model- and dataset-specific, never uniform (Table~\ref{tab:rq1}; dQWK is the
change in QWK from reasoning off to on). Reasoning
helped Qwen3.5 significantly on four of its five cells: Mohler (dQWK $+0.071$), SemEval ($+0.098$),
RIAYN ($+0.250$), and PT-CS code ($+0.136$, with the verified column agreeing at $+0.113$ on 61
items); its PT-CS short-answer gain ($+0.104$, CI $[-0.01,+0.20]$) is borderline and not
significant. The other significant cells are GLM-5.1 and GPT-5.1 on PT-CS short answers ($+0.220$
and $+0.208$) and GPT-5.1 on RIAYN ($+0.192$). Everywhere else, including every Mohler and SemEval
cell of the three discriminating models, the contrast is not significant. The verified column on the
reasoning rows rests on 25 to 67 items, so its wide intervals are precision caveats rather than new
estimates (Section~\ref{sec:datasets}).

The price has two parts. First, tokens: reasoning multiplied completion tokens by 10 to 925 times,
and the premium is domain-conditional even within one model. GPT-5.1 pays roughly 10 to 35 times on
short answers but 136 to 170 times on code; Qwen3.5, whose traces are the longest, pays 582 to 925
times (Fig.~\ref{fig:cost}). A single overall span hides exactly the conditionality this study is
about, so the framework quotes the premium per domain. Second, consistency: the within-item SD
across repetitions rises from off to on in 17 of the 20 cells (it is flat in one and falls only in
GPT-5.1's two code cells), all at temperature 0 (Fig.~\ref{fig:rq1}, Fig.~\ref{fig:consistency}).
Reasoning therefore trades reproducibility for agreement even when the agreement gain itself is not
significant; Section~\ref{sec:discussion} unpacks how much of that loss is mechanical.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig4_consistency.pgf}
\caption{The sacrificed axis: mean within-item SD across the $k$ repetitions, reasoning off versus
on, per dataset (with-guidance cells). PT-CS is the verified stratum (N=379 items).}
\label{fig:consistency}
\end{figure}

One code-specific failure shapes how the code cells must be read. On PT-CS code, Qwen3.5 with
reasoning off assigns a grade of exactly zero to roughly half of the answers (53\% of the paired
sample), a behaviour we call the \emph{zero-collapse}: the model stops discriminating at the lower
end of the scale and floors most partial-credit answers. These are genuine, parseable zero scores,
they recur on the same items regardless of scope, and the other models do not show them (GLM and
DeepSeek floor about 13\%). Reasoning repairs the collapse for Qwen (the zero fraction drops to 27\%
and agreement rises by the $+0.136$ above), which is best read as the recovery of a broken baseline,
not as evidence that reasoning helps code grading in general. Off the collapse, the code-cell gains
are inconsistent: not significant for GLM or DeepSeek on either code dataset, nor for GPT-5.1 on
PT-CS code, yet significant for GPT-5.1 on RIAYN. That below-collapse unpredictability is a finding
in its own right, and the framework (Section~\ref{sec:discussion}) keys its code guidance on
baseline behaviour rather than on model identity because of it.

\subsection{RQ2: Evaluation Guidance}
\label{sec:rq2}

\input{tables/tab_dimension_contrasts}

Guidance helps wherever the reference is trustworthy; this contrast ran on Qwen3.5 only, and every
claim here is scoped to it (Table~\ref{tab:dims}). Adding the reference answer raises agreement by
$+0.162$ QWK on Mohler and $+0.238$ on SemEval; adding the rubric raises it by $+0.123$ on RIAYN
(all significant). On full PT-CS code the rubric appears useless ($+0.005$, not significant), but
the verified column overturns that reading: on the 295 items with evidence of teacher review the
rubric is worth $+0.146$ (CI $[+0.10,+0.20]$), in line with RIAYN. The result is the pair of
numbers, not the null: an unvalidated reference masked a real rubric benefit
(Section~\ref{sec:gold}).

\subsection{RQ3: Scope and Decomposition}
\label{sec:rq3}

Neither exam-level scope nor criterion-level decomposition improved agreement, and both effects are
small (Table~\ref{tab:dims}). Grading a whole exam in one call instead of question by question moves
agreement by $-0.033$ QWK (CI $[-0.10,+0.03]$, N=252 paired questions; verified column $-0.060$ at
77, also not significant). Not significant does not mean equivalent: we ran no equivalence test, and
the interval allows effects in either direction, so the honest reading is that no harm was detected
at this sample size, not that the two scopes are interchangeable. Scoring criterion by criterion
instead of holistically yields $-0.043$ (CI $[-0.08,-0.01]$, significant, N=737), an effect that the
verified column weakens to $-0.022$ (not significant at 295): a small, gold-sensitive penalty, not a
robust harm. Both contrasts are scored against the final grade, per the design decision not to trust
per-criterion human scores as ground truth.

\subsection{RQ4: Models, Splits, and Cost}
\label{sec:cost}

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig3_per_dataset_config.pgf}
\caption{Agreement (QWK, K=5, item means, bootstrap CIs) by model and reasoning mode, per dataset,
on the with-guidance question-by-question cells. No configuration wins everywhere; the per-dataset
winner changes. PT-CS is the verified stratum (N=379 items); the GPT-5.1 bars rest on 60-item
subsets and are a reference, not a ranked competitor.}
\label{fig:perdataset}
\end{figure*}

No model wins everywhere (Fig.~\ref{fig:perdataset}). On the public datasets the open configurations
sit close together (mean QWK 0.590 to 0.667, Table~\ref{tab:ranktransfer}), and the per-dataset
winner changes: Qwen3.5 with reasoning on Mohler (0.659), GLM-5.1 with reasoning off on SemEval
(0.621), GLM-5.1 with reasoning on RIAYN (0.803). GPT-5.1 is shown beside them on its 60-item
subsets as a paid reference point; on that footing it does not dominate the open models on the
public datasets (public mean 0.643 with reasoning on), and we do not rank it.

\input{tables/tab_semeval_splits}

The SemEval generalisation splits add a calibration view (Table~\ref{tab:semeval}). With reasoning
off, accuracy is broadly stable from seen to unseen-domain and unseen-questions items (0.72 to 0.80
across models), with AUROC between 0.72 and 0.89; the unseen-answers split is the weakest for GLM
(accuracy 0.672). Discrimination of correct from incorrect answers therefore survives domain shift
better than a fixed threshold might suggest, which supports reporting the splits separately.

Cost closes the comparison, and it carries an operational lesson. The full final matrix (deduplicated
by call group) cost \euro{}35.97: \euro{}10.24 on the OpenAI anchor and \euro{}25.73 on the DeepInfra
open roster. Our a-priori estimate was \euro{}16.7, an underestimate of roughly 2.2 times (1.9 on
OpenAI, 2.3 on DeepInfra), because real reasoning traces exceeded the assumed output lengths. The
first reasoning arm had to be re-run with the token ceiling raised eightfold (4{,}096 to 32{,}768).
Practitioners budgeting a reasoning-on grading arm should expect 2 to 3 times the naive
token-estimate, on top of the per-call premium of Section~\ref{sec:rq1}.

\subsection{RQ5: Conversation State}
\label{sec:rq5}

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig5_conversation.pgf}
\caption{Conversation sub-study on PT-CS code (20 submissions, two fixed answer orders, $k=3$,
baseline configuration), per model. Left: shared history lowers the mean normalised grade relative
to a clean conversation (bias). Right: the mean absolute grade change when the same answer moves
position (variance). Qwen3.5 is read under the zero-collapse caveat of Section~\ref{sec:rq1}.}
\label{fig:conversation}
\end{figure*}

Conversation state biases grades; answer order adds noise (Fig.~\ref{fig:conversation}). Grading
inside one shared, accumulating conversation makes both models stricter than grading each answer in
a clean conversation: the mean normalised grade drops by $+0.040$ for Qwen3.5 (Wilcoxon $p=.0009$)
and $+0.027$ for GLM-5.1 ($p=.003$). Reversing the answer order within a shared conversation,
however, produces no directional effect (signed test: $p=.52$ GLM, $p=.15$ Qwen) while changing
individual grades substantially (mean absolute change 0.098 of the scale for GLM, 0.077 for Qwen):
position is variance, not bias. Because Qwen's zero-collapse can hide order effects (an answer
floored at zero cannot move), GLM is the cleaner read, and it shows the larger order sensitivity.
For deployment the implication is direct: clean, question-by-question grading is both the least
biased and the most reproducible conversation regime.

\subsection{RQ6: Transfer to the Deployment Context}
\label{sec:rq6}

\input{tables/tab_transfer_verified}
\input{tables/tab_ranking_transfer}

Transfer is partial: the top of the public ranking carries over, the middle does not
(Table~\ref{tab:ranktransfer}). The two public co-leaders among the open configurations, Qwen3.5 and
GLM-5.1 with reasoning on (public mean QWK 0.667 each), are also the two best verified short-answer
configurations (0.795 and 0.771, Table~\ref{tab:transfer}). Reasoning helps both open families on
verified short answers (Qwen 0.652 to 0.795, GLM 0.692 to 0.771, N=84 off / 32 on). Below the top,
the ranking reshuffles: GPT-5.1 with reasoning off is last on the public mean (0.566) yet strong on
the verified stratum; DeepSeek's off and on variants swap; and the best verified-code configuration
is GLM-5.1 with reasoning off (0.672 at N=295), which leads no public dataset. The GPT-5.1 anchor's
verified short-answer cells rest on 11 items, so they are directional corroboration only, never a
ranked result; their intervals (e.g.\ 0.897, CI $[0.62,0.98]$) say as much. Combined with the
per-dataset winner changes within the public set itself (Section~\ref{sec:cost}), the picture is
consistent: a strong configuration found on public data is a good starting point, and nothing below
the top is predictable enough to deploy unvalidated.

\subsection{The Gold Lesson: Reference Quality Changes Conclusions}
\label{sec:gold}

\input{tables/tab_gold_sensitivity}

\begin{figure*}[t]
\centering
\input{figures/fig6_gold_sensitivity.pgf}
\caption{Full PT-CS versus PT-CS-verified, Qwen3.5 baseline cell. Left: the same grader scores
0.308 QWK against the full gold but 0.468 against the verified stratum. Right: the rubric benefit is
invisible on the full gold ($+0.005$) and substantial on the verified stratum ($+0.146$).}
\label{fig:gold}
\end{figure*}

The same model output reads very differently against the two versions of the PT-CS gold
(Table~\ref{tab:goldsens}, Fig.~\ref{fig:gold}). Against the full, mixed-provenance gold, the
baseline grader scores 0.308 QWK; against the verified stratum, 0.468. The rubric benefit goes from
an apparent null ($+0.005$) to $+0.146$. The signed model-minus-gold deviation also grows in
magnitude ($-0.192$ to $-0.269$): an unvalidated reference does not merely add noise, it pulls
measured effects toward zero and can flip a qualitative conclusion. This sensitivity is itself a
result of the study, and it grounds the framework's governing rule: configuration findings are only
as good as the reference they are validated against.
